title: Plugin & MCP Trust Boundary Audit

findings.pattern: ...process.env passed wholesale to MCP subprocess
findings.explanation: ALL environment variables from the host process are passed to every MCP child process via StdioClientTransport. This includes secrets, tokens, and other sensitive data. The MCP config's `environment` field should be the only source of env vars for child processes.
findings.fix_suggestion: Pass only mcp.environment (user-configured) plus a minimal safe allowlist (PATH, HOME, etc). Never spread process.env wholesale.

findings.pattern: Cast of requiredCapability to any bypasses type-checked capability enforcement
findings.explanation: HOOK_CAPABILITY_MAP returns string, and checkCapability expects CapabilityId. The `as any` cast silences the type mismatch. If the map returns a non-CapabilityId string, it bypasses type checking and the runtime result is undefined (always-allowed or always-denied depending on fallback logic).
findings.fix_suggestion: Type HOOK_CAPABILITY_MAP as Record<string, CapabilityId> and add a runtime guard/assertion before the check.

findings.pattern: String literal cast to any defeats capability enum safety
findings.explanation: The string literal 'network.request' is cast to `any` before being passed to checkCapability, bypassing the CapabilityId union type. If CapabilityId enum changes, this silently breaks.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use CapabilityId.NetworkRequest instead of the string literal with `as any`.

findings.pattern: Schema.decodeUnknownOption wrapped with silent fallback to empty object on invalid auth data
findings.explanation: When mcp-auth.json contains corrupted or tampered data, Schema.decodeUnknownOption returns None. The code silently falls back to `{}` — meaning all stored tokens are silently lost with no error or log. A malicious plugin or disk corruption could wipe auth state silently.
findings.fix_suggestion: Log a warning when decode fails; attempt recovery or throw. Never silently discard auth data.

findings.pattern: pluginId is optional on ListedPlugin type
findings.explanation: The pluginId is optional throughout the plugin listing and dispatch pipeline. When pluginId is undefined, capability checks cannot map hooks back to a plugin identity. Legacy plugins that don't export an id operate without any security boundary.
findings.fix_suggestion: Make pluginId required for all security decisions. Legacy plugins without an id should get a synthetic id from their specifier.

findings.pattern: Dynamic import of third-party code typed as Record<string, unknown> with no runtime shape validation
findings.explanation: The dynamic import executes arbitrary code from the plugin entrypoint. The result is typed as `Record<string, unknown>` and then passed to `readV1Plugin(load.mod, load.spec, 'server', 'detect')` which uses `isRecord` guards and `typeof` checks. Any property not matching expected shapes is silently skipped — but the code execution itself is already complete.
findings.fix_suggestion: Plugin modules are inherently code-execution trust boundaries. Add manifest-based capability declaration before loading. Consider Worker-based isolation.

findings.pattern: Hook name accessed via as any cast on unknown hook object
findings.explanation: Hook names come from the Hooks interface, but the access is done via `as any`. If a plugin returns additional properties not in the Hooks interface, they could be invoked as hooks — potentially executing unintended plugin code.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use typed access with `hook[name as keyof Hooks]` or a typed dispatch table.

findings.pattern: HOOK_CAPABILITY_MAP cast to Record<string, string> loses CapabilityId type safety
findings.explanation: The map is already typed as `Record<string, CapabilityId>` in its definition, but recast to `Record<string, string>` here. This allows non-capability strings to slip through.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove the cast; HOOK_CAPABILITY_MAP is already typed correctly.

findings.pattern: Plugin hook function accessed via as any on the hooks array
findings.explanation: Same pattern as F07 but in the registered hooks array. Plugin hooks objects are accessed with `as any`, bypassing type safety on what functions get invoked.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use typed access — either a dispatch map or `keyof Hooks` guard.

findings.pattern: HTTP response JSON cast via `as` without Schema validation
findings.snippet: const deviceData = (await deviceResponse.json()) as { user_code: string; device_code: string; verification_uri: string; expires_in: number; interval: number }
findings.crosses_boundary: network (GitHub OAuth) → plugin code → auth provider
findings.explanation: OAuth device code response from GitHub is parsed and cast directly. If GitHub returns unexpected shape, properties will be undefined, leading to runtime crashes or auth failures with confusing errors.
findings.fix_suggestion: Define a Schema for the GitHub device code response and use Schema.decodeUnknown.

findings.pattern: OAuth token response cast via `as` without validation
findings.snippet: const data = (await response.json()) as { access_token: string; refresh_token?: string; expires_in?: number; scope?: string }
findings.explanation: OAuth token endpoint response is cast directly. A malformed response could store invalid tokens in the auth system.
findings.fix_suggestion: Define a Schema for the OAuth token response.

findings.pattern: Device code response cast via `as` without validation
findings.snippet: const deviceData = (await deviceResponse.json()) as { device_code: string; ... }
findings.explanation: Same pattern as GitHub Copilot — OAuth device code response is type-cast without Schema validation.
findings.fix_suggestion: Schema-decode the device code response.

findings.pattern: OAuth token response cast via `as` without validation
findings.snippet: const data = (await response.json()) as { access_token: string; ... }
findings.explanation: Same pattern — token response from OpenAI is cast without validation.
findings.fix_suggestion: Schema-decode the token response.

findings.pattern: Device code response cast via `as` without validation
findings.explanation: DeviceCodeResponse is an interface (no runtime validation). The `.json()` result is assumed to match the interface shape.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use a runtime schema (Effect/Schema or zod) to validate the device code response.

findings.pattern: Error body cast via `as` with .catch(() => ({})) fallback — swallows parse failures
findings.explanation: When the response body is not valid JSON, the catch returns {} and the cast assumes it's a DeviceTokenErrorBody. This silently masks transport errors during device code polling.
findings.fix_suggestion: Separate JSON parse errors from API error responses. Log parse failures explicitly.

findings.pattern: API response cast via `as` with optional chaining fallback
findings.snippet: const body = (await res.json().catch(() => undefined)) as { model_routers?: RouterEntry[] } | undefined
findings.explanation: The router list response is cast without validation. If the API returns unexpected data, model listing may be empty or crash silently.
findings.fix_suggestion: Schema-decode the digitalocean router response.

findings.pattern: JSON.parse on init.body without Schema validation — request body interception
findings.snippet: const body = typeof init?.body === 'string' ? JSON.parse(init.body) : init?.body
findings.explanation: The Copilot auth plugin intercepts and parses fetch request bodies to transform messages. JSON.parse on arbitrary input without validation can throw uncaught errors or produce unexpected shapes.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use try/catch around JSON.parse or use Schema.parseJson.

findings.pattern: TUI plugin code — not inspected in this audit, but a known trust boundary
findings.snippet: Full file not audited — mark for follow-up
findings.explanation: The TUI plugin surface (packages/plugin/src/tui.ts) handles hooks that can inject UI elements. This surface should be audited separately.
findings.fix_suggestion: Separate audit task for TUI plugin surface.

findings.pattern: Empty catch block — npa parse failures silently swallowed
findings.explanation: npm-package-arg parsing can fail for malformed specifiers. The empty catch returns undefined, which callers interpret as 'not an npm package' — but the actual parse error (malformed version range, invalid name) is lost.
findings.fix_suggestion: At minimum, return the parse error or log it. Consider returning a Result type.

findings.pattern: Empty catch during client close — errors silently discarded
findings.explanation: During MCP client cleanup, transport close errors are silently swallowed. If close fails due to a hung process or resource leak, there's no logging or alerting.
findings.fix_suggestion: Log transport close errors at minimum.

findings.pattern: Empty catch during plugin health store load
findings.explanation: Loading the plugin health store (JSON file) can fail on first run or corruption. The empty catch returns a default empty store — acceptable behavior but should log the error for debugging.
findings.fix_suggestion: Log at debug level when health store cannot be loaded.

findings.pattern: Empty catch during JWT claim parsing
findings.explanation: parseJwtClaims catches JSON.parse failures on malformed JWT tokens and returns undefined. This is intentional — invalid tokens should be treated as unparseable — but the catch is empty with no context.
findings.fix_suggestion: Consider logging at trace level for debugging malformed tokens.

findings.pattern: Empty catch during WebSocket close
findings.explanation: WebSocket close() can throw if the socket is already closed or in a bad state. The empty catch is intentional but should at minimum log at debug level.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add debug-level logging for close failures.

findings.pattern: Empty catch during WebSocket fetch body parsing
findings.explanation: parseBody tries to parse an HTTP body as JSON for WebSocket upgrade flow. The empty catch returns undefined if parse fails, which is handled downstream.
findings.fix_suggestion: Acceptable for now but consider structured logging.

findings.pattern: Empty catch during implicit token parsing
findings.explanation: OAuth implicit flow token parsing from URL fragment can fail — empty catch returns undefined.
findings.fix_suggestion: Log at debug level.

findings.pattern: Empty catch during router JSON parsing
findings.explanation: Router index parsing from local cache can fail — fallback is empty array.
findings.fix_suggestion: Acceptable fallback but log for observability.

findings.pattern: Empty catch during JWT token expiry check
findings.explanation: accessTokenIsExpiring parses JWT tokens. If parsing fails, it returns false (token not expiring). A false negative means a valid token may be treated as not expiring soon.
findings.fix_suggestion: Return true on parse failure (conservative: assume token is expiring if unparseable).

findings.pattern: Empty catch during device code error response parse
findings.explanation: Device code polling may get non-JSON error bodies. The empty catch suppresses parse errors.
findings.fix_suggestion: Log the raw body on parse failure for diagnostics.

findings.pattern: Empty catch during session fetch in subagent check
findings.explanation: Session lookup via SDK can fail — empty catch returns undefined, treating the session as non-existent.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add debug logging.

findings.pattern: Empty catch during redirect URI parsing — falls back to defaults
findings.explanation: parseRedirectUri catches URL parse errors and returns defaults. This is correct behavior but the catch is empty.
findings.fix_suggestion: Minor: log invalid redirect URIs for debugging.

findings.pattern: Schema.decodeUnknownSync used without try/catch at import site
findings.explanation: decodeModels is a sync decoder that throws on invalid input. It's called inside an async function (get()) at line 168 when the API response is parsed. If the GitHub API changes shape, decodeUnknownSync throws synchronously, crashing the model refresh flow with an unhandled exception.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use Schema.decodeUnknown (async/Effect) with proper error handling or wrap in try/catch.

findings.pattern: Direct process.env access for AZURE_RESOURCE_NAME
findings.explanation: Azure plugin reads process.env directly instead of going through the config layer. This bypasses the environment sanitization infrastructure.
findings.fix_suggestion: Route through Config layer or use sanitized environment access.

findings.pattern: Direct process.env access for CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID
findings.explanation: Same as Azure — config should be routed through the config layer.
findings.fix_suggestion: Route through Config layer.

findings.pattern: Direct process.env access for CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID (AIGateway)
findings.explanation: Duplicate of F33 in the AIGateway plugin.
findings.fix_suggestion: Route through Config layer.

findings.pattern: Direct process.env access for CLOUDFLARE_GATEWAY_ID
findings.explanation: Same pattern.
findings.fix_suggestion: Route through Config layer.

findings.pattern: Hardcoded OAuth client ID in source code
findings.explanation: OAuth client ID is a public identifier (not a secret), so this is low-risk by itself. However, hardcoding it means the client ID cannot be rotated without a code change. For public OAuth apps, this is acceptable but should be documented.
findings.fix_suggestion: If this is a public OAuth app, document it. If private, move to config.

findings.pattern: Hardcoded OAuth client ID in source code
findings.explanation: Same pattern as F36. The OpenAI Codex CLI OAuth client ID is hardcoded.
findings.fix_suggestion: Document or move to config.

findings.pattern: Hardcoded OAuth client ID in source code
findings.explanation: Reuses the Grok-CLI client ID (documented in comment). Public OAuth client IDs are not secrets but should be configurable for enterprise deployments.
findings.fix_suggestion: Document or move to config.

findings.pattern: Hardcoded OAuth client ID in source code
findings.explanation: Hardcoded DigitalOcean OAuth client ID.
findings.fix_suggestion: Document or move to config.

findings.pattern: OAuth tokens and client secrets stored in plain JSON on disk
findings.explanation: MCP OAuth tokens (access_token, refresh_token) and client secrets are stored in a JSON file at Global.Path.data/mcp-auth.json. File permissions are set to 0o600. However, the file is plain JSON — no encryption at rest. If the disk is compromised, all MCP OAuth tokens are exposed.
findings.fix_suggestion: Consider encrypting the auth store or using the system keychain (macOS Keychain, Linux Secret Service, Windows Credential Manager).

findings.pattern: Tokens schema stores access and refresh tokens as plain strings
findings.explanation: The Tokens schema stores access and refresh tokens as plain String fields. These are written to disk without encryption. A memory dump or core dump could expose tokens.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use a token type that redacts itself in logs/debug output, or encrypt at rest.

findings.pattern: ClientInfo schema stores OAuth client secret as plain string
findings.explanation: Dynamically registered OAuth client secrets are stored on disk as plain text.
findings.fix_suggestion: Encrypt client secrets at rest.

findings.pattern: OAuth client secret in user config schema
findings.explanation: Users can configure OAuth client secrets directly in opencode.json. This is a plain-text config file — secrets are stored in the clear.
findings.fix_suggestion: Support env var references or a secrets backend for client secrets.

findings.pattern: OAuth access tokens exposed in log objects
findings.explanation: The saveTokens method at line 151 logs the mcpName after saving. While the token itself is not logged directly, the code handles raw access_token strings. If logging verbosity increases, tokens could leak.
findings.fix_suggestion: Ensure token values are redacted from all log outputs.

findings.pattern: Unknown plugin ID silently denied with no logging
findings.explanation: When checkCapability is called with a pluginId that doesn't exist in the registry, it returns false silently. There's no logging or audit trail — making security misconfigurations invisible.
findings.fix_suggestion: Log a warning when checking capabilities for an unregistered plugin.

findings.pattern: Unknown hook names silently denied
findings.explanation: Hooks not listed in HOOK_CAPABILITY_MAP and not in ALWAYS_ALLOWED_HOOKS are silently denied. New hooks added to Hooks interface but not the capability map would silently stop working.
findings.fix_suggestion: Log a warning for dispatched hooks with no capability mapping — this is a configuration bug, not a normal case.

findings.pattern: OAuth config validated via typeof === 'object' instead of Schema
findings.snippet: const oauthConfig = typeof mcp.oauth === "object" ? mcp.oauth : undefined
findings.explanation: The oauth config is type-guarded with `typeof === 'object'` rather than validated with the existing ConfigMCP.OAuth Schema. Arrays, null, and other objects pass this guard but would break the OAuth provider.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use Schema.decodeUnknownOption(ConfigMCP.OAuth) for runtime validation.

findings.pattern: Duplicate: OAuth config validated with typeof instead of Schema
findings.snippet: const oauthConfig = typeof mcpConfig.oauth === "object" ? mcpConfig.oauth : undefined
findings.explanation: Same pattern as F47 in the remote MCP connection code path.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use Schema.decodeUnknownOption(ConfigMCP.OAuth).

smoking_guns.title: MCP process.env wholesale delegation
smoking_guns.why_critical: Every MCP child process receives ALL host environment variables including HOME, PATH, secrets, tokens, and API keys. This is the broadest trust boundary violation in the codebase — any MCP server (including third-party) can exfiltrate every env var.
smoking_guns.attack_surface: A compromised MCP server reads process.env and exfiltrates AWS credentials, GitHub tokens, database URLs, etc.

smoking_guns.title: Capability enforcement bypass via 'as any' casts
smoking_guns.why_critical: The hook dispatch guard — the primary security boundary between plugin code and host capabilities — uses `as any` casts on capability IDs. A typo in the capability map or a mismatch between the CapabilityId enum and the string keys in HOOK_CAPABILITY_MAP would silently bypass all capability checks.
smoking_guns.attack_surface: A plugin could register a hook name not in HOOK_CAPABILITY_MAP that still matches a CapabilityId value, or a hook name could be mistyped and bypass gating.

smoking_guns.title: Auth data silently discarded on corruption
smoking_guns.why_critical: MCP OAuth token storage decodes with Schema.decodeUnknownOption and silently falls back to empty object on failure. This means any corruption of the auth file (disk error, concurrent write, malicious tampering) results in ALL stored tokens being silently lost with no error, log, or user notification.
smoking_guns.attack_surface: An attacker who can write to the auth file can inject invalid JSON, causing all tokens to be discarded and forcing re-authentication — a denial-of-service via auth state poisoning.

smoking_guns.title: Plugin modules executed as arbitrary code
smoking_guns.why_critical: Dynamic `import()` executes third-party code in the same Node.js process with full privileges. The module's shape is typed as `Record<string, unknown>` and validated only with weak `typeof`/`isRecord` guards. There is no sandbox, no capability-based preflight, and no isolated execution context.
smoking_guns.attack_surface: A malicious npm plugin (typosquatting a legitimate plugin name) gains full process access including filesystem, network, and environment variables.

smoking_guns.title: Plugin identity is optional throughout the dispatch pipeline
smoking_guns.why_critical: The ListedPlugin type has `pluginId?: string` — optional. Legacy plugins without an `id` export have no identity in the capability system. All capability checks depend on pluginId to look up the security state in the registry. Without an id, the plugin operates in an identity-less gray zone where some checks may pass and others fail unpredictably.
smoking_guns.attack_surface: A plugin without an id may slip past capability checks that require identity, or may be silently denied access to hooks it should have.

non_audited_surfaces: packages/plugin/src/tui.ts — TUI plugin hook surface (62 exports, major surface area)
packages/opencode/src/plugin/install.ts — Plugin installation & JSONC config patching
packages/opencode/src/plugin/shared.ts:204-211 — readPluginPackage reads package.json, needs deeper schema audit
packages/opencode/src/mcp/oauth-callback.ts — OAuth callback HTTP server, CSRF protection reviewed, needs penetration testing
